channel.title: Retrospective on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Retrospective on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Fallout: Destiny Or Preparation?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about whether Fallout was destined to happen or the result of massive preparation.

This came from a conversation I had with Matt Bal in his podcast, The Sewers of Paris. I will link it below.

It’s a really good podcast and I’m a fan of Matt’s YouTube channel because he talks a lot about LGBT representation in books and movies and TV shows way long time ago and back. I mean, he does modern day, but he goes really far back and shows how there were always gay people working in a lot of the movies and TV shows that you liked.

item.title: Gaming In The 1980s
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about gaming in the 1980s.

Why do I want to talk about this? A whole bunch of reasons, but when I was doing my channel analytics a few weeks ago, I realized my biggest demographic are people between the ages of 25 and 35, which means, because it’s 2025 right now, you were all born in the 1990s, except a little sliver of you that in 2000 — let’s put that in perspective — by the end of the 9s, by 1999, I had a bachelor’s degree in computer science from an engineering school, a master’s degree in computer science specializing in Ai, and I was working on my PhD in aai, specializing in machine learning and, in particular, integrated learning. I’d also been in the game industry for almost 20 years, since 1981, and I had made several games already, including Fallout, and it’s only 1999, which is now 26 years ago. So I wanted to talk about what gaming was like in the 80s. Because it was wildly different than what it is now, and maybe by understanding some of this, or even just knowing it, you can understand some of the trends we see in gaming now. To do this, I’m going to have to probably, give contextual stories, or, for some of you, I’m going to ramble, because you have to understand gaming not only in the context of the ’ 80s, but also in context of all the other entertainment offerings in the80s, and this is going to not only talk about what the times were like, but also the social aspect of a lot of these entertainment choices and how much Focus each one got, how much time people spent in each one of these, because people have so many entertainment options now and you T talk — we’ve talked about this — all the different streaming options and all the different games. Coming out on Steam, there are so many things buying for your attention and buying for your focus that, even though you know steam is putting out, I think it we figured out was like 70 games a day.

item.title: Photos From Fallout Development (1994-1997)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a collection of Fallout photos I have from 1994 through 1997.

It’s a fun Friday. I dug these up. I figured I’d show them here now. I’ve done two videos with photos on them. One was scenes from a life in game development.

The other was snapshot throughout my career and they’re below. I also did a Fallout timeline. I’ll link that below.

item.title: Don't Forget Temple Of Elemental Evil 2003
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to ask you not to forget about Temple of Elemental Evil, my 2003 video game.

Now you’re probably going. I’m not going to forget about that. Why you? Why are you asking me not to forget about this?

Well, let me just say that I’ve talked about different reasons. I do this Channel and I think I found a new one.

I do not want this game to slip away into you — complete history — and no one ever mentions it or talks about it. Why am I bringing this up?

item.title: Temple Development Timeline
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s do the Temple of Elemental Evil development timeline.

Now a lot of people liked my Fallout and Arcanum development timelines and they’re linked below. Although it’s interesting, a lot of people comment on them, comment on them, but they’re not well watched compared to my other videos. I don’t know if it’s because they’re long, but I just wanted to point out that a lot of you say you love them, but a lot of you don’t watch them, which is a perfect example of why sometimes the vocal people are not the majority and do not represent a majority opinion. Apparently there’s a lot of you out there who think these are boring, so let me give you a caveat ahead of time. I don’t know how long this is going to be. I made a lot of notes, but keep in mind that Temple was done in half the time of Fallout and Arcanum. Those two games took three and a half years. This game took a year and a half. Plus two months, so almost exactly 50% of the time. Also, for the LA, last 20% of this game’s development.

item.title: The Canceling of Fallout Van Buren...And Me
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the cancellation of Fallout Van Buren and me.

I was watching a documentary — and I’ll link it below — on the development of interplace Fallout by bun, which was 2B, Fallout 3 — it was going to be their first 3D engine Fallout and what happened? And it was a great documentary and I was like Wow, someone’s collected a lot of the facts all in one place, but there was one fact missing that I expected to be missing, which was my involvement in its cancellation. Now this is a story I’ve held off on saying. It’s like what I’m more than a year into this Channel and I held it off for a few reasons.

item.title: Arcanum Development Timeline
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s walk through the Arcanum development timeline.

Few weeks ago — actually may even be a couple months ago — I did the Fallout development timeline. People really like that. In fact, stavos Nicola asked: can you do an Arcanum one as well? And I was like, yes, um, interestingly about doing this. So this timeline was a lot harder for me to put together than the Fallout. One was basically looking at my notes and ordering them chronologically.

item.title: Fallout 1 and 2 Box Covers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the Fallout 1 and two box covers and yes, I’m calling fallout Fallout one, just so I don’t say I’m talking about the Fallout box covers and then everyone will get confused.

Now I’ve mentioned this here and there. I know I talked about it on the why I left Fallout 2 video. I think it came up in chats with Leonard, so I’ll link those below if you want even more context. But I wanted to pull, them out, pull out that discussion and make one particular video about it. And I wanted to call it out because otherwise this is the kind of thing I think gets lost in development.

item.title: Late-Stage Troika
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about late stage troa, because I’ve told a lot of troa stories already and you can look them up.

There’s a whole troa playlist. I’ve talked about the development of all three of our games. I’ve talked about the flat hierarchy we tried to do. I’ve talked about how our games there didn’t have very good sales.

They’re all now cult classics, which is nice, I guess. Um, I told and I did an entire video of troa stories with me as the bad guy. That all happened during, so the reason I’m calling this late stage troa is I’m kind of focusing on about the last year.

item.title: Playing My Older Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about playing my older games.

There have been several suggestions people have had. They want to have me play some of my older games and stream it, or just stream.

In general, I’m not really interested in that. I mean, it’s not my thing. I don’t want that to be the Channel’s focus, and I also think there’s a bunch of much better people out there who stream themselves playing games.

item.title: Developing Pillars Of Eternity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about developing pillars of Eternity.

This has been a very well requested video, but what I put off on doing because, like South Park, I was not involved in Pillars in any lead role and my notes on it are brief. I don’t have a lot of deep notes on pillars. Luckily, you know, this wasn’t that long ago, so I can remember a lot of stuff, but it’s not like Fallout or any of the Troika games, or especially wob star carbine, where I have extensive notes on the development. But I think I can answer a lot of questions and tell you some fun stories about it, so let’s go So I was on South Park since October of 2011, when I joined with obsidian, before the kickstarter for pillars the next year, in September of 2012, I was asked to be involved.

item.title: Fallout Regrets
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer a question from commenter Brian Eno 608 better be a real name, don’t taunt me like that.

Anyway, Brian Eno asks: what’s one of your biggest Fallout related regrets, specifically story-wise, or a feature you could never get working in on time? Okay, I’m going to assume you mean development of Fallout and not like I regret walking away from the haip. So I’m going to break that into two parts because I have a different answer for Fallout one than I do for Fallout 2. Um, so for Fallout one, my biggest regret is companion AI. I’ve talked about this in another video, but companions were added to the game so late in development that there was very little time to get any AI in for them. When they were first put in, they were handled via script and I think they basically just followed the player around and shared any hostility flag that the player would have. So anything mad at the player. They would attack.

item.title: Making of Bard's Tale Construction Set (1991)
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the making of Bard’s tail construction set.

That was the first game. I made it interplay and I was a contractor. I didn’t get hired until after the game came out the way it all came about.

In late 90 my father passed away. We weren’t terribly close. My parents had divorced when I was young. But the impact of having a parent die, especially he was only 57, he’s my age, I was only. I thought I was 24, I was 25.. And I was still in grad school and it just made me rethink what I wanted to do. I was on course to become a college professor. I rethought that. I had so much fun making grandstand bridge and I’d never stopped making games.

item.title: Fallout Cut Content
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout cut content.

This has been a very frequently Asked question um. It’s. People seem very fascinated by what didn’t make it into the game and I’m going to try to answer the question. But I’ve got a big caveat here. When I left interplay, it caused some consternation in the individuals above me and I was warned multiple times that I wasn’t allowed to take any interplay property you know off the site like I would have. So that meant I left all the code behind and I left all the design documents behind.

item.title: Why I Left Fallout 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s talk about Fallout 2 and why I left it I really think, if you haven’t, you should watch yesterday’s video.

There’s a lot of context for this video in yesterday’s video about how Fallout was a b-tier project. Now I know I mentioned in that video that we had never really planned on a sequel, and by that what I meant was there were no hooks in the original story or the setting or even the system mechanics. I mean, we hadn’t planned on what would happen if the level went past 20. We just weren’t thinking of a sequel. We’re just trying to get Fallout 1 out the door. I think the only two times sequel even came up during development of Fallout 1 was during the naming meeting, where I even said vault 13 isn’t a good name because it doesn’t make for good sequels: vault 13, 2 or more vault 13 or vault 14.. And then again when we finish the manual for Fallout one, which was done.

item.title: Proposed Expansions to Vampire Bloodlines
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about two potential expansions — or you could say it’s SQL — that Troika had designed for Vampire: The Masquerade — Bloodlines.

I even found buried in one of them the fact that we were planning a trilogy, which I forgot, and there’s even a little note about what the third one would be. Before I talk about that, I just want to say, there have been so many comments I, I literally can’t keep up. I was trying to keep up. This is I’ve been doing this for just over two weeks. The first week I really did try to keep up. The second week I was falling behind. The last two days I’ve had a lot of client meetings. I have client meetings now.

item.title: Arcanum 2: Journey to the Center of Arcanum
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the sequel to Arcanum called Journey to the Center of Arcanum.

So let me give you some background. Um, for those of you new, Troika games was a game Studio that I started with Leonard barski and Jason Anderson on April 1st 1998, and our first game that we shipped was Arcanum. Arcanum shipped in 2001 using a new isometric engine we made. However, even before it shipped, we started thinking about ideas for sequels and that we wanted to make the jump to 3D. And we were talking to valve, because the VP of valve, Scott Lynch, had been the VP who signed us to Sierra for Arcanum and we were talking with them about their Source engine and we really wanted to use it. So we put together a design dock, which I still have. I read through and I’m like: Whoa, We were very ambitious. Uh, Troika liked to do a lot of things. In fact, Arcana might commonly refer to as our kitchen.
